skill.name: codebase-analysis
skill.description: Guides AI agents through systematic brownfield codebase analysis, including entry point discovery, dependency tracing, architecture pattern detection, and technical debt review.
skill.summary: Analyze unfamiliar codebases with a structured workflow for architecture, dependencies, and technical debt.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and filesystem patterns, but review found these are markdown examples for local codebase inspection, not executable skill code. The high weak-crypto and reconnaissance hits are false positives from documentation context. Publication is reasonable with a warning because the workflow asks agents to inspect files and may run shell or package-tool commands in the target repository.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many shell examples for grep, find, npm, npx, go, cargo, and other developer tools. These commands are intended for local repository analysis, but users should approve execution and review package-tool commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The markdown clearly instructs agents to run local commands as part of the workflow. The commands are normal analysis commands, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Package Tool Execution Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation suggests npx-based tools such as madge and jscpd. If packages are not already installed, npx may download and execute third-party code, which should be confirmed by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly recommend npx commands. This is common developer tooling, but it can execute downloaded package code depending on environment configuration.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Enumeration for Repository Inspection
low_findings.description: The skill recommends reading project files and listing directories to find entry points and architectural patterns. This is expected for codebase analysis, but it requires normal workspace file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access is explicit and limited to project inspection examples. No evidence found of hidden file scraping, secret collection, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Hits Dismissed as Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto findings occur on descriptive markdown and example output lines, not cryptographic implementation code. The system and network reconnaissance findings are local search examples used to identify application entry points and dependency graphs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows these are headings, tables, or benign search commands in markdown. No implementation invokes weak cryptography or performs network scanning.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on shell examples to inspect codebases. This pattern is acceptable for developer tooling but should run only inside the intended repository and with user awareness.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples, not hidden automation. They still represent a real capability request for shell execution.

content.value_statement: Unfamiliar repositories are hard to understand without a repeatable path. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through entry points, dependencies, patterns, and debt.
actual_capabilities: Guides discovery of application entry points across common languages and frameworks.
Provides commands and checklists for tracing imports, dependencies, and call paths.
Helps identify architecture patterns such as MVC, layered, hexagonal, and microservices.
Structures technical debt review by category, severity, impact, and recommended action.
Defines a standard markdown report format with optional C4 diagram output.
Includes framework reference tables for Node.js, Python, Go, Java, Rust, Dart, C, C++, and Ruby.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not an automated static analysis engine.
Command examples may need adjustment for each repository, shell, and operating system.
Some optional tools such as madge or jscpd may require installation before use.
Architecture conclusions still require human review for business context and runtime behavior.

use_cases.title: Onboard to an Unknown Repository
use_cases.description: Find entry points, project structure, major dependencies, and likely architecture before making changes.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Refactoring Plan
use_cases.description: Map dependencies, detect circular imports, and rank technical debt by severity and impact.

use_cases.title: Review Architecture Before Migration
use_cases.description: Identify framework patterns, module boundaries, and risky coupling before a rewrite or platform migration.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Repository Orientation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan for a new project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the codebase-analysis skill for a quick assessment. Identify the project type, likely entry points, primary language, framework, and next files to inspect.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Dependencies from Entry Points
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dependency tracing workflow. Start from the confirmed entry points, map direct dependencies, and summarize the most important module relationships.

prompt_templates.title: Detect Architecture Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pattern detection cookbook. Identify the primary architecture pattern, supporting evidence, secondary patterns, and any unclear areas that need human review.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Technical Debt
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the full codebase-analysis workflow with INCLUDE_DEBT enabled. Produce a prioritized debt inventory with severity, evidence, impact, and recommended remediation order.

output_examples.input: Analyze a TypeScript service before onboarding a new developer.
output: Overview with project size, primary language, framework, and architecture hypothesis.
Entry point list covering server bootstrap, routes, and CLI scripts.
Dependency summary showing direct modules, external packages, and circular dependency risks.

output_examples.input: Prepare a technical debt review for a refactoring proposal.
output: Debt inventory grouped by code, tests, dependencies, documentation, and architecture.
Severity table that separates critical blockers from medium and low priority work.
Recommended action plan for immediate fixes, sprint work, and backlog cleanup.

output_examples.input: Review architecture before moving a monolith to services.
output: Detected architecture pattern with concrete directory and dependency evidence.
Candidate service boundaries and coupling concerns.
Open questions where runtime behavior or product ownership needs confirmation.

best_practices: Start with entry points, then trace dependencies before making architecture claims.
Keep evidence linked to concrete files, directories, commands, or observed dependency paths.
Ask before running package tools or long commands in large repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not inspect random files and infer architecture without entry point context.
Do not label debt as high severity without impact, location, and remediation evidence.
Do not run npx, package managers, or broad scans without checking repository expectations.

faq.question: Does this skill modify code?
faq.answer: No. It provides analysis workflows and report formats. Any code changes require separate user approval.

faq.question: Can it analyze any programming language?
faq.answer: It covers many common stacks, but commands and architecture signals may need adaptation for niche frameworks.

faq.question: Why does it need filesystem access?
faq.answer: It must inspect project files, directories, dependency manifests, and source layout to build an accurate analysis.

faq.question: Why is there a medium security warning?
faq.answer: The skill includes shell and package-tool examples. These are legitimate but should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Does it send code to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network exfiltration or external reporting in the reviewed skill files.

faq.question: What is the best first prompt?
faq.answer: Ask for a quick repository orientation, then request dependency tracing from the confirmed entry points.
